answer:
### The Juliana Scene: Juliana's Refusal and Defense

In Cynewulf's *Juliana*, a hagiographic Old English poem, the virgin martyr Juliana of Nicomedia rejects a forced marriage to Eleusius (the "reeve," a powerful pagan official also called Heliseus in some variants), who has been selected by her father, Africanus, after lavish gifts and promises.<sup>1</sup> The poem recounts the betrothal and Juliana's refusals multiple times (with poetic repetition common in Anglo-Saxon verse), emphasizing her steadfast Christian faith over worldly authority. Here's a step-by-step walkthrough of the key scene where she defends her refusal:

1. **Betrothal by Father**: Eleusius, inflamed by lust for Juliana's beauty, woos her father with "treasure and trinkets, with tokens of gold." Africanus, enticed by the riches and status, pledges his daughter without her consent.<sup>2</sup>

2. **Juliana's Initial Refusal (to Eleusius via Messenger)**: Informed of the arrangement, Juliana sends an unbending message: she will marry him *only* if he converts—"if you bow before God... and believe in His being"—otherwise, "you cannot have me, nor force me with threats."<sup>3</sup> This prioritizes spiritual compatibility over obedience.

3. **Father Summoned and Enraged**: Eleusius, furious, summons Africanus, who returns home "in a rage" and rebukes her: obey the "richest, most righteous... noblest" suitor or face "bitter torment."<sup>4</sup>

4. **Juliana's Direct Defense to Father (First Exchange)**: Africanus pleads as her "dearest... light of my eyes," urging her not to "forsake this man’s affection." Juliana retorts: "Never will I consent... unless he worships the God of might... Otherwise he cannot bring me to his hall."<sup>5</sup> She doubles down, rejecting devil-worship and threats of death by beasts.

5. **Escalation and Second Defense**: Africanus threatens her life. Juliana replies undaunted: "I will not work a lie... You will never succeed... in turning me from the love of Christ," scorning "deaf and dumb devil-idols."<sup>6</sup>

6. **Confrontation with Eleusius**: Dragged before him, Juliana repeats: no marriage unless he forsakes idols for the "God of glory."<sup>7</sup> Tortures follow (scourging, hanging), but she prevails spiritually, binding a demon sent to tempt her, before her martyrdom.

Juliana "pays for it" with escalating torments, yet her refusals frame marriage as impossible without shared faith in the Christian God.

### Comparison to Manusmṛti Book 9

Manusmṛti (Laws of Manu) Chapter 9 outlines *dharmic* laws on women, marriage, and family, emphasizing women's perpetual dependence and the father's authority in betrothal.<sup>8</sup> No verse mandates a daughter's *absolute obedience* to her father's husband choice, but the structure subordinates her agency:

- **Father's Authority Paramount**: "Her father protects her in childhood, her husband protects her in youth, and her sons protect her in old age; a woman is never fit for independence."<sup>9</sup> Fathers must give daughters away "at the proper time" or be blameworthy; they select suitors based on excellence ("excellent, handsome, and of equal rank").<sup>10</sup> Betrothal is irrevocable once pledged ("once does one say, 'I will give'").<sup>11</sup>

- **Limited Daughter's Agency**: A post-pubescent maiden may *self-select* after three years (no sin), but without paternal ornaments (lest theft).<sup>12</sup> Father still loses authority only if he obstructs her cycles; ideal is his gift to a worthy man ("better... stay in father's house until death... than... devoid of good qualities").<sup>13</sup>

- **No Emphasis on Consent or Compatibility**: Marriage is for procreation/lineage purity; wife elevates (or degrades) with husband.<sup>14</sup> Eleusius fits Manu's worldly criteria (rich, noble), but no *dharmic* stress on mutual faith—focus is protection, not romance/ideology.

**Core Disagreement**: Not consent (neither text grants daughters full veto; Manu's is conditional self-choice, Juliana's refusal defies pledge). Not purely father's authority (both recognize it, but Juliana subverts via higher divine law). It's **spiritual vs. worldly duty**: Juliana rejects pagan marriage as soul-endangering idolatry, overriding familial/social bonds. Manu prioritizes patrilineal *dharma* (dependence, progeny) over individual will—her refusal would violate paternal protection and risk "ruin."<sup>15</sup> The corpus lacks direct analogs, but Juliana elevates Christian martyrdom over Manu's gendered hierarchy.

<sup>1</sup>: Cynewulf, *Juliana*, [Chapter 1, translation](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/).

<sup>2</sup>: *Ibid.*, [p-6bc4fe](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-6bc4fe).

<sup>3</sup>: *Ibid.*, [p-6bc4fe](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-6bc4fe).

<sup>4</sup>: *Ibid.*, [p-75ff07](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-75ff07).

<sup>5</sup>: *Ibid.*, [p-d1312b](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-d1312b).

<sup>6</sup>: *Ibid.*, [p-4a8399](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-4a8399) and [p-3fccbd](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-3fccbd).

<sup>7</sup>: *Ibid.*, [p-6a2534](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-6a2534).

<sup>8</sup>: *Manusmṛti*, [Chapter 9, translation](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/09/translation/).

<sup>9</sup>: *Ibid.*, [p-233021](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/09/translation/#p-233021) (Mn_9.3).

<sup>10</sup>: *Ibid.*, [p-7a0e13](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/09/translation/#p-7a0e13) (Mn_9.4) and [p-9a86a5](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/09/translation/#p-9a86a5) (Mn_9.88).

<sup>11</sup>: *Ibid.*, [p-5211c4](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/09/translation/#p-5211c4) (Mn_9.47).

<sup>12</sup>: *Ibid.*, [p-33ddc2](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/09/translation/#p-33ddc2) (Mn_9.90–91).

<sup>13</sup>: *Ibid.*, [p-07cdb9](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/09/translation/#p-07cdb9) (Mn_9.89).

<sup>14</sup>: *Ibid.*, [p-efa085](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/09/translation/#p-efa085) (Mn_9.22).

<sup>15</sup>: *Ibid.*, [p-3c3393](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/09/translation/#p-3c3393) (Mn_9.5).
